ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Japanese fried prawn curry rice; FOODON_00002809 (curry rice food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dish with curry sauce and fried seafood — estimated from FoodEx2 composite dish hierarchy; no exact code for ebi furai curry rice exists.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Japanese fried prawn curry rice. A00GJ (composite rice dish) is used as the closest defensible anchor. Reviewer should confirm or replace with a more specific FoodEx2 code if available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for individual prepared rice dishes of this type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of fried prawn curry rice weighs approximately 600 g and consists of two panko-coated deep-fried prawns, a generous serving of Japanese short-grain rice, and a rich Japanese curry sauce with vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — composite estimate derived from: Shrimp, coated, fried (SR Legacy 35196); Rice, white, short-grain, cooked (SR Legacy 20057); Sauce, curry (SR Legacy 06164); weighted by estimated dish composition (prawns ~15%, rice ~50%, curry sauce ~35%)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Japanese fried prawn curry rice. Values are estimated by weighted combination of three SR Legacy entries: fried coated shrimp (~15% by weight), cooked short-grain white rice (~50% by weight), and curry sauce (~35% by weight). The input record's per_100g values (energy 158 kcal, protein 5.8 g, fat 7.5 g, carbohydrate 17.5 g) are broadly consistent with this weighted estimate and have been retained with minor rounding adjustments. Sodium 300 mg/100g is plausible given curry roux sodium content. All values are estimates; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on white rice (GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate component (~50% of dish weight, ~80% of carbohydrate contribution)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Japanese fried prawn curry rice. GI is estimated at 72 (high) based on white short-grain rice (GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) as the dominant carbohydrate source. Panko breadcrumbs and curry roux contribute additional refined starch but are minor relative to rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 105) / 100 = 75.6, where carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 17.5 × 6 = 105 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from curry roux, soy sauce, and seasoning at 300 mg/100g (1800 mg per 600 g serving), approaching 78% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200–300 g, request reduced-sodium curry roux, avoid additional soy sauce, and balance the meal with high-potassium vegetables to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) driven by refined short-grain white rice and panko breadcrumbs, with low dietary fibre (0.8 g/100g), resulting in a very high glycemic load of 75.6 per standard 600 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 100–150 g, substitute brown rice or cauliflower rice where possible, limit to one fried prawn piece, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (2.5 g/100g) and trans fat (0.08 g/100g); Japanese curry roux typically contains palm oil; sodium is 300 mg/100g; and the dish generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from high-temperature frying.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to the combination of high sodium, saturated fat from deep-frying and curry roux, trans fat, and AGE formation; if consumed, limit to a half-portion and choose a curry roux made without palm oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Prawn Curry Rice (Ebi Furai Karē Raisu / エビフライカレーライス) is a popular Japanese-style rice dish served widely in Singapore's Japanese casual dining restaurants and food courts. The dish consists of two panko-coated deep-fried prawns (ebi furai) served alongside a generous portion of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, all topped or accompanied by a thick, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce made from a commercial curry roux block. The prawns are coated in flour, egg wash, and Japanese panko breadcrumbs before being deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy, yielding a crunchy exterior with a juicy, sweet prawn interior. The Japanese curry sauce is characteristically mild, slightly sweet, and viscous compared to Indian or Thai curries, typically containing onion, carrot, potato, and a commercial roux block that may include palm oil, wheat flour, and spices. Nutritionally, a standard 600 g plate provides approximately 948 kcal, 34.8 g protein, 45 g fat, and 105 g carbohydrate. The dish is energy-dense and high in refined carbohydrates with a high glycemic index (~72) and very high glycemic load (~75.6 per serving). Sodium content is significant at 300 mg/100g (1800 mg per plate), making it a concern for hypertension management. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) relevant to inflammatory conditions. This dish is not suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without significant portion modification. Known in Chinese as 炸虾咖喱饭, in Malay as Nasi Kari Udang Goreng, and widely recognised across Singapore's multicultural dining landscape.
common_user_queries: Is fried prawn curry rice healthy?
How many calories in ebi furai curry rice?
Is Japanese curry rice high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat fried prawn curry rice?
How much protein in fried prawn curry rice?
Is ebi furai curry rice suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Japanese fried prawn curry rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy entries for fried coated shrimp, cooked short-grain white rice, and curry sauce. The input record's values are broadly consistent with this estimate and have been retained. GI is estimated by analogy from white rice (University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite Japanese fried prawn curry rice dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as a single composite entry as of 2026-04-16. Values were estimated from component-level USDA SR Legacy entries (SR Legacy 35196, 20057, 06164) weighted by estimated dish composition. The input source (grabfood / foodo.sg) is not an approved primary nutrient source and its per_100g values have been cross-checked against the USDA component estimate; they are broadly consistent and have been retained with minor rounding. Trans fat 0.08 g/100g is retained from the input estimate; this is plausible for a deep-fried dish using commercial curry roux. Halal status is marked false as commercial Japanese curry roux blocks may contain pork-derived ingredients; consumers should verify the specific brand. The FoodEx2 code A00GJ is used as the closest defensible composite rice dish anchor; a more specific FoodEx2 code should be confirmed by a domain reviewer. Multilingual local names added for Chinese (炸虾咖喱饭（2只）/ 炸蝦咖喱飯（2隻）), Malay (Nasi Kari Udang Goreng (2 Ekor)), Tamil (வறுத்த இறால் கறி சாதம் (2 துண்டு)), and Hindi (तला हुआ झींगा करी चावल (2 टुकड़े)) to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for this dish as served in Singapore. Although the dish is Japanese in cuisine, it is sold in Singapore and the validator requires Singaporean-context multilingual coverage.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced with Japanese local name only.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.

Iteration 3: Added mandatory Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to local_names section to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance as required by the validator for Singapore-served dishes. Added corresponding aliases to aliases_by_language and flat aliases array for all four languages. Self-check confirmed all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases array.
